reasoning: The option-adjusted spread (OAS) of the ICE BofA US Corporate Index is currently around 0.76% (76 bps) as of late October/early November 2025, which is near historic lows for investment-grade credit spreads. Historically, US IG spreads have a long-term average closer to 130-150 bps and have very strong mean-reversion tendencies when they compress below 90-100 bps. Because the spread is constrained near its lower bound, the distribution of future movements is asymmetric to the upside (i.e., there is more room for it to rise than to fall). While short-term movements (like the 1-week horizon to Nov 16) are close to a coin toss, the probability of an increase grows with the time horizon as mean reversion exerts a stronger pull. Consequently, I assign a 54% likelihood for the spread to be higher in one week, rising to 58% at one month, and 61% at three months, reflecting the increasing probability that spreads will widen from these extremely tight levels.
